title: One global palette owns frontend and monochrome presentation
summary: The owner controls C/C++/CUDA presentation through one Dans palette: the frontend is a global aggregate, monochrome is a separately remembered preference, and an enabled frontend forces monochrome without erasing that preference.
details: :Dans is the canonical interactive surface and <leader>dan is an alias; lower-level Dans commands remain stable automation and compatibility APIs rather than a memorization requirement.
Frontend presentation is one global choice applied to every loaded and future C, C++, and CUDA buffer. The owner palette deliberately does not expose individual renderer toggles.

The frontend requires effective monochrome so provenance and ownership colors are not mixed with an unrelated syntax palette. Its monochrome row is therefore locked in buffers where the frontend renders, not in unrelated C# or Python contexts.

Requested monochrome remains independent global state. It directly controls C#/Python presentation and controls C/C++/CUDA whenever the frontend is off; a frontend-on C++ buffer alone uses the forced effective value.

Disabling the frontend means source-faithful spelling, including disabling the declaration/decorator aggregate, documentation markdown, project macro coloring, scope coloring, and an active tokenizer view. It does not rewrite buffer bytes.
Font size and every owner-facing workflow otherwise registered as a Dans command are reachable from the same palette.
Font-size input accepts values from 8 through 32 points inclusive. Invalid or out-of-range input is rejected without changing guifont.
rationale: A grid of per-feature toggles was unusable for the owner and allowed presentation modules to drift into combinations that were neither source-faithful nor the accepted frontend.
The C++ classic-syntax base links are process-global, while language-qualified Tree-sitter captures make the requested C#/Python palette truthful across simultaneously visible buffers.
Separating requested from effective monochrome preserves a real owner preference while expressing the frontend's color dependency as a visible lock rather than silently destroying state.
A discoverable palette makes new tools usable without requiring command-name recall and gives tests one inventory boundary for future commands.
alternatives: Keep the old per-module two-column toggle grid; rejected because the owner explicitly found it unusable and does not want individual feature toggles.
Keep a one-dimensional vanilla switch; rejected because source-faithful monochrome and source-faithful normal-color views are distinct useful states.
Make the stored preferences buffer-local; rejected because the owner chose one remembered palette, while buffer context only determines whether the frontend forces or exposes that preference.
Remove the underlying Dans commands; rejected because stable programmatic entry points remain useful even when they are not the human interface.

provenance.note: Owner required one discoverable menu, a whole-frontend toggle, locked monochrome while the frontend is active, retained font control, no individual feature toggles, and access to all Dans workflows.
Owner selected an inclusive 8 through 32 point range and required every other size to be rejected.

Neovim's classic syntax base groups are global while conceal, extmarks, and language-qualified Tree-sitter groups permit buffer-language-specific effective presentation over one remembered preference.
Owner required monochrome disabling to work for C#/Python and the frontend menu row to be gray outside the explicit C/C++/CUDA suffix set.

evidence.claim: The real menu drives C++ and Python contexts through normal-color, forced-monochrome, locked/unlocked interaction, disabled frontend activation, and preference restoration without changing source bytes.

questions: Why can monochrome not be toggled in a C++ buffer while the frontend is on?
Does turning the frontend off restore normal C++ source and colors?
Why are individual frontend modules absent from the Dans menu?
Is display mode global or buffer-local?

last_verified.method: Owner decisions implemented and exercised through isolated real-Neovim contextual menu, language-palette, font-boundary, and renderer interactions.
